explaining the outcomes in the linear regressions, such as in the case
of the disability indicator and the "temporary visa or refugee"
variable, which did not present statistical significance in the
categorical version of the model for "there are too few jobs available
for me in my ideal commuting time." We decided to use the categorical
models because they enabled us to identify levels of satisfaction in a
non-normative way.

Regarding the relationship between perceived and calculated accessibility, only transit accessibility, in one of the models, presented the behaviour hypothesized in which increasing accessibility was associated with more positive perceptions. Although the active accessibility indicator presented a slightly positive net effect for active commuters in both models, this effect was not sufficient to outweigh the influence of the other explanatory variables. As a result, the estimated active thresholds were excessively demanding and unrealistic from a policy perspective. For one model, we were unable to identify thresholds, while for the other, the conservative threshold was 22 times higher than the median accessibility level and the progressive threshold was more than 123 times higher than the median accessibility. These results led us to propose equity standards based solely on the model "there are too few jobs available for me in my ideal commuting time," and for public transit mode. 

For future research, we suggest exploring additional place-based accessibility measures to further evaluate the relationship between calculated and perceived accessibility. For instance, the raw multimodal spatial availability indicator could be tested. This indicator uses as output the number of jobs accessible by different transport modes, not standardized by the number of people using these modes. We decided to use multimodal spatial availability per person because we believe that competition matters when evaluating perceptions related to employment outcomes, as demonstrated by previous studies [@jin2018; @merlin2017;
@miller2005]. Another limiting factor that led us to adopt the CT level was that we could not estimate travel times at the DA level for the entire study area due to the memory limitations of our computational resources. Despite that, the initial tests performed with a smaller study area did not show differences in terms of the sign and statistical significance of the accessibility coefficients between regressions - probably because the indicators are standardized.

Continuing with the spatial unit used, the CTs, we recognize that this
unit is not the most detailed among census geographies, being less
detailed than dissemination areas or dissemination blocks. We decided to
use CTs due to the large size of the study area, in order to provide a
regional analysis rather than focus on only one city, considering this
entire basin to calculate the accessibility indicators and select the
sample. This choice is motivated by the recognition of commuting flows
across municipalities, which are even one of the parameters used by
Statistics Canada when defining Census Metropolitan Areas.

Regarding the equity standards definitions, three moral principles guided our approach. The first relies on the recognition of accessibility as a human capability [@sen2005; @sen1999; @pereira2017; @luz2022], something required for a dignified life [@karner2020], and the main transportation benefit to be distributed across society in order to increase people's freedom. The second is grounded in the sufficientarian perspective, considering a fair transportation and land-use system as one that provides sufficient accessibility to all members of society [@martens2021] as requirement for them to meet their basic needs [@pereira2017; @pereira2021]. The third moral principle is grounded in an egalitarian perspective, in which the minimum level of accessibility is defined in terms of the most equity-deserving population groups to reduce inequalities of opportunities. Making explicit the moral foundations that support our analysis is, in our perspective, important because different theories of justice lead to different perspectives on how policies should manage the distribution of transport-related benefits. 

Transport equity is only one step towards achieving transport justice
[@karner2020]: a condition in which no person or group is disadvantaged
by a lack of access to the opportunities they need to lead a dignified
and healthy life. To achieve this state, it is necessary to transform
the structures and processes that lead to the unequal distribution of
the multiple externalities of transport across population groups and
space. In this study, we propose a methodology to suggest equity
standards based on a data-driven approach. We intend to suggest minimum
levels of accessibility that could guide transportation planning towards
a more equitable distribution of transportation benefits, as an earlier
stage to be succeeded by a participatory and democratic process to
collectively select the best standard, considering that residents and
other stakeholders must be able to actively participate in and influence
the decisions that affect their lives. While this is, in our
perspective, the most appropriate method for standards definition, it is
beyond the scope of this study to develop democratic plenaries to
discuss the suggested standards.

A positive aspects from this research is that the equity standards are
suggested based on their link with perceived accessibility, making them
easier to communicate with policy practitioners and the general public.
We also accounted for possible endogeneity of calculated accessibility,
so that the coefficients represent the true effect of the indicators and
not a mere a correlation. Although the study does not ensure full
reproducibility, since the used surveys are confidential, it ensures
replicability by making all codes available for consultation. 

# Conclusions

The main objective of this study was to suggest equity standards for
transportation planning based on the relationship between calculated job
accessibility and perceived accessibility referred to employment
outcomes, for a study area covering municipalities around the cities of
Toronto and Hamilton, southern Ontario, Canada. For that, we used data
from the 2021 Census of Population and the recently released National
Survey on Transport Poverty [@tiznado-aitken], combining place-based calculated
accessibility measures with variables at the individual level, while
controlling for endogeneity using instrumental variables in a two-stage
regression estimation.

The place-based accessibility models were created using multimodal
spatial availability per person, considering walking and cycling (later
combined as active transportation), public transit, and motorized
transportation modes. In general, our models showed that these
indicators partially predicted with statistical significance perceptions related to how easy it is to reach work and declining employment opportunities because of transport situation. For the
model regarding the perception of having too few jobs available within
an ideal commuting time, public transit accessibility behaved according
to our hypothesis that increasing calculated accessibility would reduce
negative perceptions, in which one additional job accessible by public
transit per transit user increases the average odds of being in a more
positive perception group by 25% (p-value \< 0.05). Our models also
showed (p-value \< 0.05) that, for active users, active accessibility
has a slightly positive effect on individual perception, increasing their
odds of being in a positive category. Using the "few jobs available" model and for
the public transit mode, we suggest equity standards based on the
accessibility threshold that, results in a higher likelihood of positive
perceptions for people from equity-deserving groups. We suggest three
equity standards: a conservative standard (0.16 jobs reached by public
transit per transit user), an intermediate standard (0.26), and a
progressive standard (0.36).

Our results show that most of the study area falls short of
the proposed equity standards, regardless of their category
(conservative, intermediate, or progressive). About 66% of the study
area population lives in areas with transit accessibility below the
conservative standard, 91% in areas below the intermediate standard, and
almost the entire study area lives below the progressive standard.

Finally, we believe our study contributes to the ongoing discussion of
equity in transportation, and that equity standards based on, or
inspired by, our methodology can serve as a data-driven starting point
for debate among residents and other stakeholders within a participatory
and democratic process to collectively define the most appropriate
standard.

we created this paper using literate programming in which the R markdown
code to fully reproduce this article is available on our GitHub
repository *(include after the review)*.

# Declaration of competing interest {.unnumbered}

The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

# Appendix {.unnumbered}

```{r}
impedance_functions <- read.csv(paste0(here(), "/manuscript/tables/impedance_functions.csv")) %>% 
  mutate(pcd_label = case_when(PCD == "3518" ~ "Durham", 
                               PCD == "3519" ~ "York", 
                               PCD == "3520" ~ "Toronto", 
                               PCD == "3521" ~ "Peel", 
                               PCD == "3524" ~ "Halton", 
                               PCD == "3525" ~ "Hamilton"), 
         distribution = case_when(
    distribution == "lnorm" ~ "Lognormal",
    distribution == "gamma" ~ "Gamma",
    distribution == "exp" ~ "Exponential",
    distribution == "unif" ~ "Uniform",
    distribution == "norm" ~ "Normal",
    TRUE ~ distribution), 
    across(c(est_1,est_2), ~ round(., digits = 2)), 
  across(c(AIC), ~ round(., digits = 0)), 
           est_2 = case_when(
    distribution == "Exponential" ~ NA, 
    TRUE ~ est_2)) %>% 
  dplyr::select(pcd_label, PCD, PwMode,distribution, est_1, est_2, AIC) %>%
  kable(format = "latex",
        booktabs = TRUE,
        caption = paste0("\\label{tab:impedance_functions}Impedance functions calibrated for the census subidvisions of the study area."), 
        col.names = c("Subdivision", "Census Code","Mode", "Impedance function","Parameter 1*","Parameter 2*", "AIC")) %>%
    kable_styling(full_width = FALSE,  
                latex_options=c("scale_down"),
                font_size = 9) %>% 
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, align = "c") %>% 
  collapse_rows(columns = 1, valign = "top", latex_hline = "major") %>%
  kableExtra::footnote(general = c("For each probability distribution, Parameter 1 and Parameter 2 correspond to their standard defining parameters: the mean and standard deviation (for lognormal and normal), the rate and shape (for gamma), and the power (for exponential). The AIC represents the Akaike Information Criterion used to assess model fit."), threeparttable = T) %>%
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

\renewcommand{\thetable}{A.1}

```{r echo=FALSE, cache=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

impedance_functions
```

```{r}
# Linear Models
few_jobs_linear_model <- readRDS(paste0(standards_measures_diretorio,"few_jobs_linear.rds"))

decline_employment_linear_model  <- readRDS(paste0(standards_measures_diretorio,"decline_employment_linear.rds"))

# Formulas 
FewJobs_formula_3mode_lm_SA <- formula(few_jobs_linear_model)
DeclineEmployment_formula_3mode_lm_SA <- formula(decline_employment_linear_model)

single_row_models <- c("Match_mode", "Indigenous_or_racialized", "Temporary_or_refugee", "LongTermConditions", "Woman_or_genderdiverse", "TransitAvailable", "TransitPass", "SA_pp_Active_IV:Active_user", "SA_pp_Transit_IV:Transit_user")

fj_linear_model_table <- few_jobs_linear_model %>% 
  tbl_regression(show_single_row = single_row_models, 
                 label = label_list, 
                 intercept = TRUE) %>% 
  add_global_p() %>% 
  modify_header(label = "**Variable**") |>
  add_significance_stars(hide_p = FALSE, hide_ci = TRUE,  pattern = "{estimate}{stars}") %>%
   remove_footnote_header(columns = estimate) %>% 
  modify_table_body( ~ .x %>% dplyr::mutate(significant = p.value < 0.05)) %>%
  modify_table_styling(columns = estimate,
    rows = significant == TRUE,
    text_format = "bold")

de_linear_model_table <- decline_employment_linear_model %>% 
  tbl_regression(show_single_row = single_row_models, 
                 label = label_list, 
                 intercept = TRUE) %>% 
  add_global_p() %>% 
  add_significance_stars(hide_p = FALSE, hide_ci = TRUE,  pattern = "{estimate}{stars}") %>%
  remove_footnote_header(columns = estimate) %>% 
  modify_table_body( ~ .x %>% dplyr::mutate(significant = p.value < 0.05)) %>%
  modify_table_styling(columns = estimate,
    rows = significant == TRUE,
    text_format = "bold")


combined_table_linear <- tbl_merge(tbls = list(fj_linear_model_table, de_linear_model_table),
    tab_spanner = c("**Few jobs**", "**Decline employment**"))

combined_table_for_printing_linear <- combined_table_linear |>
  as_kable_extra(format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE, full_width = TRUE, caption = "\\label{tab:linear_models}Weighted linear regression models connecting calculated to perceived accessibility." ) |>
  kableExtra::add_footnote("*p<0.05; **p<0.01; ***p<0.001", escape = FALSE, notation = "none")
```

\renewcommand{\thetable}{A.2}

```{r}
combined_table_for_printing_linear
```

# References {.unnumbered}
